stem: A 68-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 20 mg weekly and prednisolone 5 mg daily presents with worsening fatigue. She had a flare two months ago managed with a prednisolone pulse. She has no overt bleeding, no melaena, no menstruation since age 52 and a normal colonoscopy three years ago. She has no chronic kidney disease (eGFR 76) and no malignancy. Her appetite is preserved and BMI is 26 kg/m^2. She takes paracetamol regularly and avoids NSAIDs because of methotrexate. She has no overt source of blood loss. On examination she has active synovitis of MCPs and wrists with mild conjunctival pallor and no organomegaly. There is no telangiectasia and no hepatomegaly. She lives independently and is a retired nurse. She has had no recent transfusion. Her ESR is 78 mm/h and CRP 42 mg/L. The registrar wonders whether she has iron deficiency superimposed on chronic disease anaemia. She wants to know whether she should start oral iron.
lead_in: Which result pattern most strongly indicates that iron deficiency is contributing in addition to anaemia of chronic disease?

options.text: Normocytic indices with ferritin 142 micrograms/L in isolation
options.rationale: Ferritin 142 in isolation suggests adequate stores, but in active inflammation ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and can be falsely normal; this finding alone does NOT identify combined deficiency (BCSH IDA Guideline; eTG Haematology).

options.text: Low serum iron with normal TIBC and elevated ESR
options.rationale: Low serum iron with normal/reduced TIBC is the classic ACD pattern alone; this does not establish combined iron deficiency without additional markers (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Hb 98 g/L with active synovitis, suggesting any anaemia in this context must be iron deficiency
options.rationale: ACD alone can produce a normocytic anaemia at Hb 90-110 g/L; clinical inflammation is not specific for combined IDA without confirmatory iron studies (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Transferrin saturation 12% AND elevated soluble transferrin receptor
options.rationale: In active inflammation ferritin is unreliable; transferrin saturation under 20% with elevated soluble transferrin receptor (which is NOT acute-phase modified) reliably indicates true iron deficiency superimposed on ACD. This pattern justifies iron replacement, ideally parenteral given inflammation reduces oral absorption (BSG IDA 2021; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: MCV 84 fL and low reticulocyte index
options.rationale: Normocytic indices with hypoproliferative reticulocytes are characteristic of ACD itself; this pattern does not differentiate combined IDA from pure ACD (BCSH IDA Guideline 2021).

stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been receiving prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since post-op day 1 plus an unfractionated heparin infusion for 48 hours intraoperatively and post-op. He develops new right calf swelling, tenderness and a confirmed proximal DVT on ultrasound. His platelet count has fallen from 280 x10^9/L pre-op to 78 x10^9/L today, a nadir 72% drop. There is no bleeding. He has no skin necrosis at injection sites and no thrombocytopenia of other obvious cause - no sepsis, no DIC, no recent IV contrast. The blood film shows no schistocytes. He has not been septic. He has not received recent platelet transfusion. The cardiothoracic registrar calculates a 4Ts score of 7 (thrombocytopenia 2, timing 2, thrombosis 2, no other cause 1). The lab confirms PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is sent. He has no contraindication to alternative anticoagulants. He has CKD with eGFR 42. He is asking what should happen next.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Stop heparin and observe; restart heparin once platelets recover
options.rationale: Stopping alone leaves the patient in a prothrombotic state with high risk of new venous and arterial events; rechallenge with heparin is contraindicated (ASH 2018).

options.text: Continue enoxaparin and add aspirin
options.rationale: All heparins (including LMWH) must be stopped immediately when HIT is suspected at a high probability score; aspirin is inadequate (ASH 2018).

options.text: Start warfarin immediately to bridge while heparin is withdrawn
options.rationale: Warfarin must NOT be started in acute HIT until platelets recover above 150 because of risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis from protein C depletion (ASH 2018).

options.text: Transfuse platelets to correct thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: Prophylactic platelet transfusion is contraindicated in HIT unless major bleeding is occurring, because it can worsen thrombosis (ASH 2018; ANZSBT).

options.text: Stop all heparin AND start a non-heparin anticoagulant (e.g. fondaparinux or argatroban) at therapeutic dose
options.rationale: HIT is a prothrombotic, life-threatening condition. Stopping heparin alone is INSUFFICIENT - patients require therapeutic-dose non-heparin anticoagulation. Fondaparinux is acceptable off-label in stable patients; argatroban is preferred in renal/critically ill (ASH HIT Guideline 2018; eTG).

stem: A 42-year-old previously well non-smoker presents to ED with one week of progressive facial swelling, neck swelling, dyspnoea and headache worse on bending forward. She has noticed dilated veins on her chest wall. She has had two episodes of pre-syncope in the last 24 hours. She has no haemoptysis or chest pain. On examination she has facial plethora, periorbital oedema, distended jugular veins to the angle of the jaw, dilated collateral veins on the anterior chest wall and a positive Pemberton sign. Stridor is absent at rest. There is no airway compromise currently but she is sitting forward in bed and saturating 95% on 2 L O2. CT chest with contrast shows a large anterior mediastinal mass compressing the SVC with extensive thrombus. She has not been previously diagnosed with malignancy. She has no fever or features of infection. She has no smoking history. Her FBC, U&Es, LFTs and LDH (elevated at 950) are pending tissue diagnosis. She has good IV access. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids. She is haemodynamically stable. She has no airway stridor at rest but the ED team are concerned about deterioration.
lead_in: In the absence of airway compromise or critical neurological symptoms, which step is most important BEFORE starting empirical corticosteroids?

options.text: Start dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat regardless of biopsy timing
options.rationale: Empirical steroids before biopsy are appropriate only with airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms; otherwise they may obscure lymphoma histology (eviQ SVC).

options.text: Start mediastinal radiotherapy empirically before tissue diagnosis
options.rationale: Radiotherapy without histology obscures pathology, can render large areas uninterpretable, and the regimen depends on tumour type. Reserve for life-threatening compression with no other options (eviQ).

options.text: Obtain tissue diagnosis (core biopsy of accessible peripheral node or mediastinal biopsy)
options.rationale: In SVC obstruction without airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms, tissue diagnosis should be obtained BEFORE corticosteroids whenever possible because steroids can transiently shrink lymphoma and obscure histology, delaying definitive treatment by weeks. Anterior mediastinal mass + young non-smoker with high LDH = lymphoma until proven otherwise (eviQ SVC obstruction; BSH Lymphoma).

options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation alone for the SVC thrombus
options.rationale: Anticoagulation may be added for thrombus, but it does not address the underlying tumour; tissue diagnosis remains the priority (eviQ).

options.text: Endovascular SVC stenting as definitive management
options.rationale: SVC stenting provides rapid symptomatic relief and is appropriate for refractory or critical SVCO, but it should not delay tissue diagnosis when the patient is clinically stable; histology guides chemotherapy choice (eviQ).

stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis is admitted to ED with massive haematemesis. He has bled approximately 2.5 L by the time he arrives and is in haemorrhagic shock. He has no known allergies. He is haemodynamically unstable - BP 78/42, HR 142, peripherally shut down, GCS 13. The ED activates the massive transfusion protocol. He has received 6 units of packed red cells, 4 units of FFP and 1 pool of platelets over the last 60 minutes via rapid infuser. He is being continued on the MTP. Repeat bloods show Hb 78 g/L (after transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10-1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62. ECG shows tall T waves in V2-V4. He has good central access. The blood bank is keeping pace. He has not received tranexamic acid yet within this episode. He has no known cardiac arrhythmia. He has no contraindication to calcium replacement. The bleeding source is being controlled endoscopically.
lead_in: Which is the single most important next intervention to address an immediately life-threatening complication of massive transfusion?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL (or calcium chloride 10% 10 mL via central access)
options.rationale: Citrate in stored blood products chelates ionised calcium causing acute hypocalcaemia that depresses myocardial function and worsens coagulopathy. Ionised calcium 0.84 is severe and immediately life-threatening when combined with ongoing transfusion. Calcium replacement (gluconate peripherally or chloride centrally) is the single most important next intervention; it also helps stabilise myocardium against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP; NBA Patient Blood Management).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct acidosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not correct the underlying lactic acidosis of hypoperfusion; the priority is restoring perfusion, replacing calcium and addressing hyperkalaemia. Bicarbonate also acutely lowers ionised calcium, worsening the hypocalcaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Insulin 10 units IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is reasonable for hyperkalaemia, but ionised calcium 0.84 with continuing massive transfusion is the more immediate life-threat because it impairs cardiac contractility and coagulation. Calcium also stabilises the membrane against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Stop the massive transfusion protocol pending haemodynamic improvement
options.rationale: He remains in haemorrhagic shock with ongoing bleeding; stopping MTP risks exsanguination. Address metabolic complications IN PARALLEL with continued resuscitation (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: TXA should be given (within 3 hours of bleeding onset) for major haemorrhage and is part of MTP, but it does not address the immediate cardiac risk of ionised calcium 0.84 plus hyperkalaemia; calcium correction is more urgent for this stem (ANZSBT MTP).

stem: A 62-year-old man with a bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement five years ago for severe aortic stenosis presents with three weeks of low-grade fever, malaise, anorexia and a 4 kg weight loss. He had a dental scaling six weeks ago without antibiotic prophylaxis. He has no penicillin allergy. On admission, T 38.2 degrees C, HR 92, BP 132/78. He has a soft early diastolic murmur not present at his last cardiology review. There are two small painful nodules on his left index finger pad and a single splinter haemorrhage. There is no embolic stroke on examination, no skin emboli to soles. Two of three blood cultures drawn 12 hours apart grow viridans group streptococci. A transthoracic echocardiogram suggests mild aortic regurgitation; transoesophageal echocardiography demonstrates a 10 mm mobile vegetation on the bioprosthetic valve and an early perivalvular abscess. ECG on admission shows PR 168 ms with normal QRS. He has commenced empirical IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly plus IV gentamicin per eTG. Renal function is normal (creatinine 78 micromol/L). He is haemodynamically stable. Cardiac surgery has reviewed and plans urgent valve replacement within 72 hours.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate additional monitoring step over the next 72 hours?

options.text: Weekly CT chest with contrast to screen for septic pulmonary or systemic emboli
options.rationale: Imaging for septic emboli is driven by symptoms, not weekly routine; this does not address the conduction-system risk in aortic root abscess (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Single daily 12-lead ECG on the ward round, no continuous telemetry monitoring required
options.rationale: Daily ECG can miss intermittent block evolving over hours. Continuous telemetry detects evolving heart block in aortic root abscess (BMJ Best Practice: Infective endocarditis).

options.text: Twice-weekly serum vancomycin trough levels for therapeutic drug monitoring
options.rationale: He is on penicillin and gentamicin, not vancomycin; gentamicin troughs are appropriate but do not screen for conduction-system extension (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Daily transthoracic echocardiography to track vegetation size and perivalvular extension
options.rationale: Serial TTE has lower sensitivity than TOE for perivalvular extension and is not the recommended monitoring strategy; surveillance is by clinical examination, telemetry and repeat TOE if features change (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Continuous ECG / telemetry to detect progressive PR prolongation or AV block
options.rationale: Aortic root abscess extends into the conduction system and progressive PR prolongation or new AV block predicts perivalvular extension and need for urgent surgery. Telemetry is mandatory until source is controlled (BMJ Best Practice: Infective endocarditis; eTG).

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a first unprovoked left popliteal DVT confirmed on Doppler ultrasound. She has had two prior first-trimester miscarriages and one mid-trimester foetal death, all with no anatomical or chromosomal cause identified. Her sister has SLE. She is on no regular medications, smokes 5 cigarettes per day, drinks no alcohol, and uses copper IUD contraception. She has no other thrombotic risk factors and no recent travel or immobilisation. On examination she is well, BP 124/76, HR 78, with the tender swollen left calf, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods sent: lupus anticoagulant positive on dilute Russell viper venom time at presentation and again 12 weeks later, anti-cardiolipin IgG strongly positive at both timepoints, anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I IgG positive at both timepoints. FBC normal, UEC normal, LFTs normal, ANA positive at 1:160 with anti-dsDNA negative and complements normal. APTT mildly prolonged that does not correct on 1:1 mixing. She has been commenced on therapeutic LMWH and a long-term anticoagulation plan is being formulated, with a working diagnosis of primary antiphospholipid syndrome.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: DOACs (especially rivaroxaban; less robust evidence with apixaban) are associated with higher recurrence rates than warfarin in APS, particularly triple-positive APS, and are not recommended (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR target 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely
options.rationale: In triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome with venous thromboembolism, warfarin with target INR 2.0 to 3.0 is the recommended long-term anticoagulant. DOACs are inferior in APS, particularly triple-positive disease (EULAR APS 2019; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily monotherapy for life
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient for secondary prevention after VTE in APS; full-intensity anticoagulation with warfarin is required (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR target 3.0 to 4.0
options.rationale: Higher-intensity warfarin (INR 3-4) does not reduce thrombotic recurrence over standard intensity (INR 2-3) in venous APS, while increasing bleeding risk; standard intensity is preferred for venous events (EULAR APS 2019).

options.text: Three months of LMWH then cessation of anticoagulation
options.rationale: Unprovoked VTE with triple-positive APS carries a high recurrence rate; indefinite anticoagulation is recommended unless contraindicated (EULAR APS 2019).

stem: A 64-year-old man presents with a 24 hour history of an exquisitely painful, hot, swollen right knee. He has had three prior episodes of acute right first metatarsophalangeal joint pain in the past 4 years, each lasting 5-7 days and self-managed with NSAIDs. He is on hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension, has chronic kidney disease (eGFR 38), and a history of duodenal ulcer 2 years ago on proton pump inhibitor maintenance. He has no fever today but reports brief rigors 12 hours ago. He has no recent skin breaks, no IV drug use, no overseas travel and no immunosuppression. He has no penicillin allergy. On examination, T 37.8, HR 92, BP 142/86. The right knee is held in slight flexion, with a tense effusion, marked warmth and erythema spreading 4 cm beyond the joint line. Active and passive movement is severely restricted by pain. There are no other joints involved. Bloods: WCC 12.4, CRP 142, urate 0.52 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L, eGFR 38. Joint aspiration is performed: cloudy yellow fluid, leukocyte count 28 000 cells/microL with 85 percent neutrophils, Gram stain negative, polarised microscopy reveals negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Cultures are pending.
lead_in: What is the single best acute pharmacological management while awaiting cultures?

options.text: Empirical IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly without diagnostic joint aspiration or microbiology samples
options.rationale: Joint aspirate already shows crystals with negative Gram stain and the clinical picture fits gout; empirical antibiotics await culture rather than treating without indication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Oral indomethacin 50 mg three times daily for 5 days with a proton pump inhibitor for gastroprotection
options.rationale: NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated with eGFR 38 and a prior duodenal ulcer; indomethacin in particular has high renal and GI risk (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Allopurinol 300 mg orally daily started immediately during the acute flare to lower the serum urate
options.rationale: Urate-lowering therapy is not started during an acute flare unless the patient is already established on it; initiating allopurinol acutely can prolong the flare. It is started after the flare settles, with concurrent prophylactic colchicine cover (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

options.text: Intra-articular methylprednisolone 40 mg plus oral colchicine 1 mg then 500 micrograms 1 hour later
options.rationale: Negatively birefringent needle crystals plus prior podagra confirm gout. With eGFR 38 and recent peptic ulcer, NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated. eTG and BMJ Best Practice support intra-articular steroid in monoarticular gout with low-dose colchicine (cap 1.5 mg total in 24 hours per eTG, with dose reduction for renal impairment). The negative Gram stain supports gout, but await culture before fully excluding co-existing septic arthritis (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology; BMJ Best Practice: Gout).

options.text: Oral colchicine 1 mg six times daily continued until profuse diarrhoea develops as a dosing endpoint
options.rationale: Old high-dose colchicine regimens are discarded because of toxicity; current dosing caps at 1.5 mg in the first 24 hours, with renal dose-adjustment. Provoking diarrhoea is not a dosing endpoint (Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology).

stem: A 58-year-old man with CKD stage 5 not yet on dialysis (eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m^2 due to ADPKD) is reviewed in nephrology clinic. He is asymptomatic of bone pain or pruritus but has cramping. He follows a low-phosphate diet with the renal dietitian. Medications: amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, sodium bicarbonate 840 mg TDS, cholecalciferol 1000 IU. He is not yet anuric. Bloods at today's visit (consistent with the trend over the last 3 months): corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L, phosphate 1.95 mmol/L (rising from 1.5 three months ago), PTH 78 pmol/L (rising from 45), 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L. He is engaged in dialysis education and a fistula has been formed. He is being worked up for kidney transplantation.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in managing his CKD-MBD?

options.text: Commence oral calcium carbonate 600 mg with each meal as a phosphate binder
options.rationale: Calcium-containing binders add to overall calcium load and are associated with greater progression of vascular calcification in CKD; KDIGO 2017 recommends restricting their use, particularly when other binders are available (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Start oral calcitriol 0.25 microgram daily to suppress the rising PTH
options.rationale: Activated vitamin D analogues raise both calcium and phosphate via gut absorption; starting calcitriol with phosphate 1.95 mmol/L will worsen hyperphosphataemia and accelerate vascular calcification. Phosphate control must come first (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Commence oral cinacalcet 30 mg daily as a calcimimetic
options.rationale: Cinacalcet (a calcimimetic) is PBS-restricted to dialysis patients in Australia and lowers calcium; with corrected calcium already 2.18 mmol/L hypocalcaemia is a risk. It is not first-line in non-dialysis CKD (Source: PBS - cinacalcet; AMH).

options.text: Add a non-calcium phosphate binder (sevelamer) and reinforce dietary phosphate restriction
options.rationale: KDIGO and KHA-CARI advise lowering phosphate towards the normal range as the first lever in CKD 5 with rising phosphate and PTH. A non-calcium binder is preferred over calcium-based binders because of vascular calcification risk, particularly with a calcium near the lower limit (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017 Update; KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

options.text: Refer urgently for subtotal parathyroidectomy given the rising PTH
options.rationale: Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe medically refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism, typically PTH persistently above approximately 88 pmol/L (800 pg/mL) despite optimal medical therapy with hypercalcaemia/hyperphosphataemia or symptomatic disease; medical therapy has not yet been escalated (Source: KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

stem: A 38-year-old woman is admitted with three days of fatigue, easy bruising, and confusion. She had a vague URTI two weeks ago, denies diarrhoea, and is not pregnant (LMP one week ago). Medications: nil regular. No new drugs or supplements. Examination: T 37.8, BP 146/90, HR 104, GCS 14 (oriented to person, intermittent disorientation to place), petechiae on shins, mild jaundice, no lymphadenopathy. Bloods: Hb 78 g/L with schistocytes on blood film, platelets 18 x10^9/L, LDH 1820 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, indirect bilirubin elevated, DCT negative, INR 1.0, APTT normal, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (was 70), urea 12, troponin mildly elevated, urinalysis blood 1+ protein 1+. Stool culture for Shiga toxin-producing E. coli has been sent but is not yet back. ADAMTS13 activity has been sent. She has no history of malignancy, no recent transplant or chemotherapy.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before ADAMTS13 results return?

options.text: Await stool Shiga toxin PCR and ADAMTS13 activity before any TMA-specific therapy
options.rationale: Withholding treatment for TMA pending results is unsafe; untreated TTP carries > 90% mortality. Empirical plasma exchange is the standard of care when ADAMTS13 deficiency is clinically likely (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of platelets to raise the platelet count above 50 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in suspected TTP can precipitate further microvascular thrombosis and is contraindicated unless there is life-threatening bleeding or a procedure is required (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Start eculizumab as the first-line therapy for presumed complement-mediated TMA
options.rationale: Eculizumab is the treatment of choice for atypical HUS (complement-mediated), suggested by predominant renal injury without severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. The clinical picture here is TTP-predominant; eculizumab is considered only if ADAMTS13 is preserved and aHUS is diagnosed (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Commence urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and high-dose corticosteroids for empirical TTP
options.rationale: MAHA + severe thrombocytopenia + neurological involvement + cardiac involvement + only modest renal dysfunction is the textbook TTP phenotype. ADAMTS13 results take time and TTP has > 90% untreated mortality, so empirical plasma exchange and steroids start immediately on clinical suspicion (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020; BSH 2023).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis-related disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.rationale: DIC is unlikely with a normal INR/APTT and normal fibrinogen; the picture is non-immune MAHA + thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation, which is the haematological signature of TMA, not DIC (Source: ISTH DIC vs TMA review).

stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of harmful alcohol use is brought to ED after being found drowsy. She has eaten poorly for two weeks. Examination: GCS 11, BP 110/70, HR 96, RR 14, T 36.5, weight 58 kg, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology, no asterixis. Bloods on arrival: Na 108 mmol/L, K 3.0, urea 2.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.2, serum osmolality 232 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 460 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 38 mmol/L, TSH normal, random cortisol normal. She is not on diuretics; her only regular medication is thiamine (irregularly taken). She has had one self-limited generalised tonic-clonic seizure since arrival. No history of CCF, cirrhosis or recent surgery; no SSRI, no carbamazepine.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV hypertonic saline (3%) 150 mL boluses, capping the 24-hour sodium rise at 8-10 mmol/L
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, GCS 11) warrants rapid initial correction with 100-150 mL hypertonic saline boluses to relieve cerebral oedema, but the 24-hour rise must be capped at 8-10 mmol/L (and 18 mmol/L over 48 h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. This is doubly important here given malnutrition and harmful alcohol use, which are additional ODS risk factors (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014; ANZICS / Australian intensivist statements).

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 250 mL/h with hourly sodium measurement
options.rationale: Isotonic saline is too slow to address acute cerebral oedema with seizures and may paradoxically worsen hyponatraemia in SIADH (de-saltation effect). Hypertonic saline is the agent of choice for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Free water restriction to 800 mL/day as the sole acute intervention
options.rationale: Water restriction is appropriate for asymptomatic chronic SIADH but is inadequate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure; rapid initial correction with hypertonic saline is needed first (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally to block V2 receptors and aquarese free water
options.rationale: Vaptans risk over-rapid correction and ODS, especially in alcoholic malnourished patients; they are not recommended in severe symptomatic hyponatraemia at presentation (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Allow spontaneous correction by treating the underlying cause aggressively over 24 hours
options.rationale: Uncontrolled correction is the precise mechanism that produces ODS; an explicit cap on the 24-hour rise (8-10 mmol/L) is mandatory in high-risk patients (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).
